---
title: "IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    code-fold: false
lightbox: true
---

![High-resolution SPHERE image of the dusty disk around the young star IM Lupi, revealing fine structures in scattered light. Credit: ESO/H. Avenhaus et al./DARTT-S collaboration.](images/im_lupi_gallery.jpg){#fig-imlup fig-align="center" width="650"}

As shown in @fig-imlup, high-resolution scattered-light observations reveal the dusty disk surrounding the young star IM Lup.

## Object names

| Catalog | Name |
|---|---|
| Primary name | IM Lup |
| Other designations | Sz 82 |
| Region | Lupus 2 cloud |

## Location

IM Lup is associated with the **Lupus 2 cloud** and lies at a distance of **161 ± 10 pc** [@cleeves2016]. Its location in a nearby star-forming region and its young age make it a useful system for studying how gas and dust evolve differently during the early stages of protoplanetary disk evolution.

## Overview of the system

IM Lup is a young, massive, and extended protoplanetary disk whose gas and dust components show markedly different spatial distributions [@cleeves2016]. ALMA observations of the continuum, **$^{12}$CO**, **$^{13}$CO**, and **C$^{18}$O** were used together with physical and chemical modeling to constrain the structure of the disk.

A key result of the paper is that the **millimeter dust disk is truncated at about 313 au**, while the **gas disk extends to at least 570 au and perhaps as far as 970 au** [@cleeves2016]. This contrast indicates that large grains are more radially compact than the gas, consistent with dust growth, settling, and radial drift.

The study also found tentative evidence for weak ring-like substructure in the millimeter continuum and showed that the disk is both **massive** and **flared**, properties that may be related to its young age [@cleeves2016].

## General system properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Distance | 161 ± 10 pc |
| Age | 0.5–1 Myr |
| Spectral type | M0 |
| Stellar mass | ~1 M☉ |
| Stellar luminosity | 0.9 L☉ |
| Disk inclination | 48° ± 3° |
| Position angle | 144° ± 3° |

## Disk morphology

ALMA observations show that IM Lup has a **two-component millimeter dust structure**: a bright inner disk and a broad outer dust halo truncated at **313 au** [@cleeves2016]. In contrast, the gas traced by CO isotopologues is much more extended and appears smoother in radial distribution.

The continuum data also show shallow deviations from a smooth radial profile, suggesting **tentative ring-like substructure**. These features are weak and were not resolved as sharply as the rings later seen in higher-resolution disk surveys, but they are still morphologically important because they hint that substructure may already be present in IM Lup [@cleeves2016].

The paper describes these features as small breaks in the deprojected continuum brightness profile near **150 au** and **250 au**.

## Morphology measurements

| Structure | Type | Radius (AU) |
|---|---|---|
| Inner bright disk | Bright central component | unresolved inner disk, within ~21 AU model component |
| Outer dust edge | Dust truncation radius | 313 ± 15 |
| Ring candidate 1 | Tentative ring-like break | ~150 |
| Ring candidate 2 | Tentative ring-like break | ~250 |
| Gas disk extent | Extended gas disk | at least 570 |
| Gas disk extent (possible maximum) | Extended gas disk | ~970 |

## Dust in the disk

Dust in IM Lup was observed with ALMA continuum data at **1.3 mm** and **875 μm** [@cleeves2016]. These observations trace the distribution of large, millimeter-sized grains, which are more concentrated than the gas and appear sharply truncated at **313 au**.

The continuum emission is dominated by a bright central component plus a broad outer dust distribution. The paper models the large-grain dust disk as being more radially compact and more vertically settled than the gas. This is consistent with the idea that large grains have already evolved significantly through **growth**, **settling toward the midplane**, and **radial drift** [@cleeves2016].

The inner dust disk is also inferred to be **optically thick**, which helps explain the reduced line emission seen near the center in some CO isotopologue maps.

## Dust properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Continuum wavelengths | 1.3 mm, 875 μm |
| Total dust mass | 1.7 × 10⁻³ M☉ |
| Large-grain outer radius | 313 ± 15 AU |
| Dust settling parameter ($\chi_{\rm bulk}$) | 0.25 |
| Grain populations | Small grains + millimeter grains |
| Inner disk component size | 21 AU |
| Inner surface density enhancement factor | 4 |

## Gas in the disk

The gas component of IM Lup was observed in **$^{12}$CO**, **$^{13}$CO**, and **C$^{18}$O** transitions [@cleeves2016]. These lines show that the gas disk is much more extended than the millimeter dust disk.

The paper finds that the gas disk extends to **at least 570 au** and possibly to **~970 au**, making IM Lup one of the clearest examples of a large dust–gas size difference in a protoplanetary disk [@cleeves2016].

The gas structure appears relatively smooth compared with the dust continuum. In addition, the central low-velocity channels of **$^{12}$CO J = 2–1** show a diffuse outer component or halo, which may be associated with extended gas beyond the main disk.

## Gas properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular tracers | $^{12}$CO, $^{13}$CO, C$^{18}$O |
| Gas disk extent | at least 570 AU |
| Gas disk extent (possible maximum) | ~970 AU |
| Model gas outer radius | 1200 AU |
| Estimated gas mass | ~0.17 M☉ |
| CO reduction relative to ISM value | ~factor of 20 |
| Gas scale height at 100 AU | 12 AU |
| Characteristic radius of gas ($R_c$) | 100 AU |
| Gas surface density exponent ($\gamma$) | 1.0 |

## Evidence of planet formation

At this stage, **Cleeves et al. (2016) do not provide direct evidence for embedded protoplanets in IM Lup**. However, the paper does identify processes that are strongly connected to early planet formation.

The strong difference between the compact millimeter dust disk and the much larger gas disk is consistent with **grain growth, settling, and radial drift**, which are key steps in the evolution from dust grains to planetesimals [@cleeves2016]. The tentative ring-like breaks at **~150 au** and **~250 au** are also morphologically interesting, although the authors emphasize that higher-resolution observations are needed to confirm them.

For now, this paper provides **indirect evidence of dust evolution relevant to planet formation**, rather than direct evidence of planets opening gaps.

## Physical interpretation of IM Lup

Cleeves et al. (2016) interpret IM Lup as a young and massive disk in which gas and dust are already evolving differently [@cleeves2016]. The large grains are more compact radially and more settled vertically than the gas, while the gas remains extended far into the outer disk.

The study concludes that the dust structure can be understood as a bright inner disk plus a broad outer halo truncated at **313 au**, whereas the gas extends to much larger radii. This contrast supports a picture in which the solid component is undergoing substantial evolution, even though the system is still very young.

The paper also suggests that the disk may retain some surrounding diffuse material, since the low-velocity **$^{12}$CO** emission includes a halo-like component that could be related to a remnant envelope, captured material, or other extended gas structures. Even so, the main morphological result remains the same: **IM Lup shows a clear decoupling between the spatial distributions of gas and millimeter dust** [@cleeves2016].

## Activity

**Coming soon**

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
